content:
Building AI systems that can show their work — and defend it

3 min read
Just now

--

The previous post of this pair established why being right is not the same as being trusted. This post is about what to put in place so that trust can actually be built and defended. Not a technical specification — a leadership checklist for the decisions that need to be made before any AI system touches a consequential workflow.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Paige Prevost on UnsplashWhat should the system be able to show?
The first requirement is source attribution. An AI system operating in an enterprise workflow should be able to show where its answer came from — which documents, which data, which retrieval results contributed to the output. Not as a reporting feature added after the fact. As a built-in property of how the system generates responses.
There is an important distinction here between citation and attribution. A system can cite a source it never meaningfully used. Attribution requires a verifiable link between the output and the material that shaped it. In regulated environments — legal, financial, healthcare, compliance — that link is what auditors will ask for first. If the system cannot provide it, the organisation is left holding accountability it cannot discharge.
Alongside attribution, the system should surface its reasoning path and signal uncertainty honestly. A system that produces uniform confidence regardless of how weakly grounded its answer is not explainable — it is persuasive. There is a meaningful difference. Confidence thresholds, uncertainty flags, and chain-of-thought outputs are not cosmetic features. They are what allow a human to interrogate an output rather than simply accept it.

What should the organisation be able to reconstruct?
Explainability at the model level is not sufficient on its own. The organisation also needs to be able to reconstruct what happened after the fact — which model version ran, what was retrieved, which tools were called, what the system was shown before it responded, and what action followed.
Most teams log outputs. Very few log the full decision context alongside them. Without that, post-incident investigation becomes guesswork and regulatory defence becomes very difficult. According to a Dataiku and Harris Poll survey from 2026, 85% of CIOs report that explainability gaps have already delayed or stopped AI projects from reaching production. The problem is not capability. It is the absence of the right evidence when scrutiny arrives.
One thing most teams do not account for is explanation drift. A system that was explainable at deployment may not be explainable six months later. As models are updated, retrieval layers change, and knowledge bases evolve, the reasoning behind outputs shifts even when the outputs themselves look similar. Explainability needs to be re-validated continuously — not established once and assumed to hold.

What should leaders demand before deployment?
Two things that are non-negotiable before any AI system goes near a consequential decision.
The first is contestability. A defensible AI system is not just one that can explain itself. It is one that allows the humans working with it to challenge the output, invoke an override, or escalate when the explanation is insufficient. Reading an explanation is not the same as being able to act on doubt. Contestability means the human has a real path to push back — and that path was designed in, not discovered after the first challenge arrived.
The second is treating explainability as a control architecture, not a reporting layer. Most organisations still approach explainability as a dashboard or a compliance document attached to a model before launch. That is the wrong mental model. Explainability designed as a control spans model behaviour, source provenance, audit trails, uncertainty signalling, and contestability — all the way through the system, not bolted on at the end.
The teams that get this right will move faster, pass audits more cleanly, and earn adoption in workflows where opaque systems stall. The ones that treat it as a checkbox will hit the same trust ceiling described in the previous post — not because the model is wrong, but because nobody can defend it when it matters.

Explainability and auditability are not the final layer. Once a system can be depended on, secured, and explained — the next question is who controls it, and when. That is the focus of the next pillar in this series: Human Control and Operational Boundaries.
